title: Sanitize NaN, Infinite, -Infinite causing error when saving as PostgreSQL JSON
body:
Bug fix: Redash executor crashes when query output includes NaN/Infinity/-Infinity because PostgreSQL JSON/JSONB (and Redash’s JSON serialization settings) do not allow out-of-range float values. Stacktrace shows failure during INSERT into query_results.data.

Root cause: after #6685 removed simplejson (which used ignore_nan=True), Redash now uses `redash.utils.json_dumps` with `allow_nan=False`, which raises `ValueError: Out of range float values are not JSON compliant` when encoding data containing NaN/Inf.

Desired behavior: sanitize NaN/Inf/-Inf to `None` (JSON null), matching JavaScript `JSON.stringify` behavior. Add unit tests and confirm manually with example BigQuery/PostgreSQL queries.

step_plan.summary: Prevent Redash workers from crashing when query results include NaN/Infinity/-Infinity by recursively sanitizing non-finite floats to JSON-null (Python None) before PostgreSQL JSON/JSONB persistence. Add/adjust serialization and persistence coverage so query results save and round-trip successfully with allow_nan=False.

steps.operation: Add recursive sanitization of non-finite floats (NaN/Infinity/-Infinity) to None before JSON encoding, while keeping allow_nan=False
steps.reason: `json.dumps(..., allow_nan=False)` raises `ValueError` when the payload contains NaN/Inf. Centralizing a recursive sanitizer in `json_dumps` ensures any Redash code path that relies on this helper (DB persistence, caching, API responses where used) produces JSON-compliant output and matches JavaScript `JSON.stringify` behavior (non-finite numbers -> null).
steps.side_effects: Changes serialization semantics globally for callers of `json_dumps`: NaN/Inf/-Inf will silently become `null` instead of erroring. This may affect any downstream logic that previously depended on the exception or on preserving NaN/Inf tokens (which are invalid JSON anyway). Add/adjust unit tests that assert conversion and ensure performance is acceptable for large result payloads.

steps.operation: Ensure DB-bound JSON serialization path cannot write non-finite floats by relying on updated json_dumps (and/or adding a final defensive sanitize pass before returning)
steps.reason: This is the SQLAlchemy boundary used to serialize `QueryResult.data` into Postgres JSON/JSONB-compatible text. Even if other code bypasses `json_dumps`, this method must guarantee JSON compliance so inserts/updates never crash during flush.
steps.side_effects: If additional sanitization is added here, it may duplicate work already done by `json_dumps` (minor CPU overhead). Behavioral change affects all columns using `JSONText`: persisted JSON will contain nulls for non-finite floats rather than raising. Requires confirming round-trip decode produces Python `None`.

steps.operation: Sanitize runner output before storing it into QueryResult.data (if maintainers prefer a narrower or defense-in-depth fix at the query-results boundary)
steps.reason: `store_result` is where raw query-runner results are attached to the model before persistence. Applying sanitization here provides defense-in-depth and makes the behavior explicit for query results, even if other storage paths later change. This also helps keep `QueryResult.data` consistently free of non-finite floats in memory (not only at serialization time).
steps.side_effects: May increase memory usage and CPU due to deep transformation of result payloads at store time (in addition to serialization). Could slightly change in-memory behavior for callers that inspect `QueryResult.data` prior to commit. If sanitization is already fully handled in `json_dumps`/`JSONText`, this step might be redundant—ensure tests reflect intended single-source-of-truth to avoid double-processing.
